sanskrit_devanagari:
वासांसि जीर्णानि यथा विहाय
नवानि गृह्णाति नरोऽपराणि ।
तथा शरीराणि विहाय जीर्णा-
न्यन्यानि संयाति नवानि देही ॥ २२ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
vāsāṁsi jīrṇāni yathā vihāya
navāni gṛhṇāti naro 'parāṇi |
tathā śarīrāṇi vihāya jīrṇāny
anyāni saṁyāti navāni dehī || 22 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical; vedabase renders without explicit pāda breaks but body text matches).
exact (transliteration body identical; Mukundananda house style adds h-aspirate marks on śarīrāṇi → śharīrāṇi etc., but akṣaras match).
exact (text body identical; punctuation rendering differs).

exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.022 = MBh_06,024.022). Note: this verse is in trishtubh meter (11 syllables per pāda, 44 per shloka) — distinct from the surrounding anushtubh.

translations.verbatim_quote: As a person puts on new garments, giving up old ones, the soul similarly accepts new material bodies, giving up the old and useless ones.
As a person sheds worn-out garments and wears new ones, likewise, at the time of death, the soul casts off its worn-out body and enters a new one.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Change of body by the atomic individual soul is an accepted fact. Even the modern scientists who do not believe in the existence of the soul, but at the same time cannot explain the source of energy from the heart, have to accept continuous changes of body which appear from childhood to boyhood.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Yatha, as in the world; vihaya, after rejecting jirnani, wornout; vasamsi, clothes; narah, a man; grhnati, takes up; aparani, other; navani, new ones; tatha, likewise, in that very manner; vihaya, after rejecting; jirnani, wornout; sarirani, bodies; dehi, the embodied one, the Self which is surely unchanging like the man (in the example).
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the simile precisely: as a man (narah) is unchanging while changing clothes, so the embodied Self (dehī) is unchanging while changing bodies. The simile carries both halves — the actor in the simile (the wearer of clothes) is the unchanging substrate, just as the dehī is the unchanging substrate across body-changes. The verse names body-change as analogous to clothing-change, and asserts the wearer's identity is preserved across both.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: That those who give up their bodies in a righteous war get more beauteous bodies than before, is known through the scriptures. Casting off worn-out garments and taking new and beautiful ones, can be only a cause of joy, as seen here in the world in the case of new garments.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads the verse pragmatically: clothing-change is normally a happy event — old worn rags off, new garments on — therefore body-change should be received the same way. The simile is not just a logical illustration of the Self's continuity (Shankara's emphasis); it is also a re-framing of grief itself. What grieves the unenlightened is the loss of the worn-out cloth; the realised one sees the cloth-change as occasion for satisfaction, not lament.

positions.rendering: the soul accepts new material bodies, giving up the old and useless ones
the soul casts off its worn-out body and enters a new one
the embodied Self (dehī) is the unchanging substrate, like the man (narah) in the clothes-example
clothes-change is normally a happy event; body-change should be received the same way (re-framing grief, not just continuity)

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Modern translations capture the surface simile (cast off worn-out cloth, take new) but flatten two distinct doctrinal stakes. Shankara emphasises that the simile establishes the dehī's continuity-of-identity across body-changes (the verse is a continuation of 2.20-2.21's atman-as-imperishable). Ramanuja additionally emphasises that the simile re-frames the affect of body-change: as new clothes are usually a satisfying gain, the loss of an old body is no occasion for grief. The two readings are compatible but emphasise different doctrinal moves — continuity vs. affect-reframing. The translations elide both.

literal_meaning: As a man, casting off worn-out garments, takes up other new ones, so the embodied Self, casting off worn-out bodies, enters into other new ones.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The famous body-as-clothing simile. Krishna gives the imperishable doctrine of 2.19-2.21 a homely illustration: the dehī (embodied one) is to its succession of bodies as a man is to his succession of clothes. Three things follow: (1) the wearer's identity is preserved across body-changes; (2) the loss of one body is no more grievous than the loss of one garment; (3) the verse is a corrective to Arjuna's grief over the kinsmen he is about to fight — their bodies will fall, but what is bodied (dehī) is not lost. The simile appears in trishtubh meter, breaking the chapter's anushtubh — a metrical signal that this is the pivot illustration of the imperishable block.
